talk to me about, like, editorial quality and how you judge editorial
Real quality, whether a piece is high quality or low quality, how do you know it's not good enough?
Talk to me a little bit about your process for evaluating that.
So I think of editorial quality in terms of clarity first.
Like, is the piece clear?
Line editing and all of that, you can use AI, but is the piece clear?
And then that's normal thing.
are there logical gaps?
Do the ideas in the piece ladder up to each other?
Will somebody read this piece and say, you're making sense from that piece?
And this goes all the way back even to stuff like a crypto narrative.
Like I tell the analysts, you don't have to be right all the time.
If you were right all the time, we'll be millionaires doing crypto research.
But you have to be logical all the time.
I have to read the piece and say, it makes sense, even if I'm not sure to make me money.
The first thing is clarity.
The next thing is then, does this connect to the intent, which is the business intent?
So let's say we have a strategy.
The strategy already maps out to the business need of the client.
And that business need maps out to an audience need.
The audience need maps into the content teams.
The content teams then map into the specific pieces.
So we're taking specific pieces.
And then for me, I then walk back into, if I take the specific piece, does it lead us back into a specific content team that we had in the brief?
If it lead us into that brief, does it lead us into an audience need?
If it lead us into the audience need, does it lead us into the business impact?
So each of those lead us tells me whether this is a good piece.
Beyond just looking at, is the grammar correct?
The grammar is correct.
It's like the baseline of editing.
For me, it's sharpening the ideas.
If everything lead us up all the way back to business impact.
That's the way I think about it.
Whether it's a high quality piece or not a high quality piece.
Yeah, got it.
That's great.
And can you describe, so bring that like kind of grounded a little bit.
Can you talk to me about like an editorial process or content process that you improved at any of the places that you have worked and kind of walk me through how you improved it?
Yeah,
You noticed a gap for improvement there and then worked to improve it.
Let's start with CryptoNary.
So when I joined CryptoNary, it's really a talented team of researchers and crypto analysts and all of that.
And they had two editors.
The problem was, number one, there was no process.
So some people emailed in their drafts.
Some people send it in a random Google Docs.
Some people just go ahead and publish.
Which, the researchers took the editorial feedback as suggestions.
They took deadlines as suggestions.
The editors felt the researchers were undisciplined.
The editors felt the editors knew nothing about Crypto, which is actually right.
And so there was this mutual disrespect.
And then I came in as head of editorial.
And I saw that they saw, I had to first bring everybody on the same page, culture-wise.
Like, people are paying £50 a month for the subscription.
These are not...
Just followers on Twitter.
They are customers.
are paying customers.
We need to maintain a certain level of standard if we want them to keep paying.
So bring everybody on the same page, editors and researcher.
Then tell the editors you have to brush up on your crypto fundamentals standard.
And then for the researchers, deadlines and editorial feedback are not suggestions.
They actually keep up.
And I removed everybody's access to publishing.
You can't just publish now.
You have to start going through the process.
That's number one.
Number two was then, I then started with the editorial guideline.
So this is what we expect to see in each piece that you send to the editor.
And then I created a bunch of checklists as well.
I did that in my test assignment as well.
But that's just like a compressed version of how I think about it.
So even you as the writer, you look at your checklist yourself before you send it over to the editor.

And then if the editor disagrees and says, this piece is not good enough, the editor can actually leave notes on the checklist on why you did not get this point in the checklist and send it back to you.
So...
It removes that subjective part of editing that makes people feel, this editor is just out to frustrate my life.
Using checklist, eliminate that because you can see point blank, what you are missing, what you're getting right.
So I introduced the idea of checklist.
I introduced the idea of style guides as well because, and that's really common knowledge.
Like if people don't know what good is, it's hard for them to actually aspire to it.
And I think in my experience, the biggest friction point is when everything sounds subjective.
So you can't, the editor can tell you this is wrong, but he can't tell you why it's wrong.
I think people always have a problem with that.
So I think having the guides written down, it could evolve, it could change over time, but there should be something that you can mark it against.
It can't just be how you're feeling.
Some days, even when you're the editor, you wake up on the wrong side of the bed, and you don't want that to impact how you judge the writer's work.
So that's why I designed it.
So I had the style guide, the editorial checklist, and then all these rules about how things flow.
So then I then said, let's actually rework the process.
So let's have the...
Idea, the ideation phase, let's have a review.
So when you have an idea, just don't go and write the piece.
Let's talk about the idea of what you want to write, and let's approve the idea.
Then you go write the piece, and then we'll review the piece as well and approve it.
So I introduced a checkpoint from ideation to drafting, because it's also feel bad when you spend so much, three days, four days, writing a piece, and you bring it.
I'm like, nah, we can't publish it.
That's not good enough.
So from ideation, we review, you make a case for why you want to write on why, the bullish case for aerodrome, and you make the case to me, and it makes sense.
And we say, okay, go ahead.
It doesn't make sense.
Let's find another idea.
Review at that point, and then we draft, and then we review.
Then I started this thing I carried over from Animals, where we do 30% draft, 90% draft.

So the 30% draft for relatively new writers who are not yet familiar with the process, instead of them going full fire, full steam, and writing a full draft, they do a 30% draft, which is basically bullet points.
of what they want to do, and then we then look at that, okay, everything makes sense, then they go ahead to do a 90% drafts, which then comes to the editor before we get to final polish.

I did that and took what looked like an obvious blog into actually a production-ready publication that people were confident paying 50 pounds a month to use, and that's when they moved me from head of editorial to, okay, come and do more beyond the textual reports to actually everything content, video, videos, events, live streams, and of that, and then they moved me from head of editorial to head of content.
That's kind of like an example I can give.

bought Bitcoin at 150.

But the other thing I think is, if you connect the idea of what you're trying to change in a way that people can actually connect with it, if you explain an article in a way that they can connect with it, it doesn't sound like they're just trying to make their life better, but they see it.

Where do you or how do you understand when good is good enough?
Like talk to me about your decision making process because deadlines are deadlines.
Things do have to be shipped.
Nothing is ever perfect.
So what's your kind of rubric, personal rubric for understanding like, okay, this is good enough.
Like how do you judge that?
So I think it starts from actually the brief writing point.

If we write the brief well and the brief is well articulated and there is no clarity issue like the writer understands the brief, it's good to arrive at a shared understanding of whether this is good enough or not and then leveraging all those checklists.
That said, the way I think about it is I look at business impact and relationship risk.
So let's say we have to publish a piece and that piece is tied to a client.
I'm using Grotex, for example, and that piece is tied to a revenue event for the clients.
They're launching a new product and they need a fresh release that goes with that piece, with that launch.
It has to be ready.
That's not a deadline we can ship.
We can ship deadline on behalf of the client.
That's one.
Let's say we have a new client that we just signed up last week and we're onboarding them this week and they have a piece going down next week.
We can start up and say, sorry, that your first week is going to get delayed three days.
Or a client that is already marked like at risk, maybe they've been complaining too much.
That's not it.
So I look at all those.
If those things are in place, then we have to move even on at and make sure that we deliver high quality when we said we will deliver it.
If there is no business impact or relationship risk, then we might have a little leeway when a draft is not quite up to standard.
okay, maybe we need an extra 24 hours and we communicate that with the client.
But if there is a business relationship risk in place, if it means me rolling up my hands and doing that stuff myself, I actually roll up my sleeves.

Do it myself just to contain the immediate risk, and then we then look at the systematic or process issues that led to that lapse in the first place, and then try and figure out how we prevent that from happening again the next time.

So I know, like, growthx is relatively new and all of that, and I'm trying to understand, like, this is actually super cool, trying to use, like, embrace the use of AI without handing over our reasoning to AI, like, using it, like, as a competent partner.
But I'm still trying to understand, like, how do you measure success in a role like this for managing editors?
If you can share some insights on that.
Yes.
I think there's a couple of major ways that we measure it.
Obviously, this is not going to come as any surprise, I wouldn't think, but the quality of content and the satisfaction that the client has with the content is part of the measurement.
You have to meet client expectations in terms of the outcomes for the content.
So we deal with clients that are wildly variable in terms of sophistication, editorial sophistication.
I'm sure you've seen it, where some people are just incredible judges of content and other people are like, hey, my expertise is elsewhere.
That's why you're here.
And so you really cannot rely on, oh, do they think this piece is high quality or low quality or good or bad or whatever?
And in all reality, even if they are amazing judges of content and they pay really close attention and you're meeting those and all that's good, none of that matters without outcomes.
So we are a very outcome oriented company.
going company.
we're

When it comes to delivering for the client, we don't view the win as, hey, we met our deadlines, they got content, the boxes are ticked, all of this column and air table is full, like all these documents are there.
That's not success.
That is a stepping stone.
Like, sure, success doesn't happen without that.

But then the success is the feedback loop of, okay, we're 90 days into this engagement, we're three months into this engagement, we're six months into this engagement, we spoke to them about goals that they wanted to achieve, how are we tracking to those goals?

And obviously, we keep ourselves honest on a week-to-week basis, so we're not surprised by this, but on those client checkpoints, whether they are monthly check-ins, weekly check-ins, quarterly, or the big one, contract renewal time, you know, with the 12 months or whatever, have we been tracking towards their goals?
Have we been meeting goals?
And the keeping yourself honest part of it in the near term is...
If we are consistently meeting our deliverables, we are generating content that we said we would, and we are shipping it, all of that.

If we are tracking the results part of it, whether that is conversions, traffic, top of funnel, just overall traffic, or qualified traffic, whatever it is, AEO optimization or visibility, SEO visibility, whatever goals we talk to them about, if we're not meeting those, the proactive stance internally is super vital, because you have to say, hey, I know we're saying we're doing everything we said we were going to do, and we're meeting our contractual obligations to deliver this content, but it's not working.
It's not actually producing the desired outcome.
And that's why we're here.
That's why we hopefully are leveraging AI workflows to kind of lift the raw.
It's of, say, deep research as just one example, where doing deep research on a topic takes an enormous amount of time.
And it's not as simple as throwing a query of perplexity and then doing one or two drill downs.
That's not enough.

It needs to be really specific, directed research that dives deeply into understanding all of the questions that you want to ask and have answered to fully support the writing of an article based on a brief.
So if you give, say, a human researcher a brief about a highly technical topic, let's say it's, you know, hey, I really want a deep guide to L2s.
And I want you to understand everything about the rails that L2s run on, the relationships to the L1 chains and how they have evolved over time.
And then I also want you to sort of like create a map of their ecosystem and really write a comment.
